---
title: "Translations"
description: "Offer your menu in multiple languages. Diners pick their language from the menu header."
sidebarTitle: "Translations"
icon: "language"
---

Rumizi runs one menu in as many languages as you want. You translate the
parts diners actually read — item names, descriptions, and category names —
and Rumizi handles the switching.

## Languages Rumizi supports today

English, Spanish, Italian, Arabic, Hindi, Persian (Farsi), French, Chinese,
Punjabi, and Tagalog. Right-to-left languages (Arabic, Persian) render
right-to-left automatically.

## Set up your languages

<Steps>
  <Step title="Open Settings → Languages">
    In the workspace, go to **Settings → Languages**.
  </Step>
  <Step title="Pick your default language">
    This is what diners see if they haven't chosen anything else, and what
    fills in when a translation is missing.
  </Step>
  <Step title="Add supported languages">
    Choose every language you want diners to be able to switch to.
  </Step>
  <Step title="Save">
    Once you save, the **Translations** tab appears inside every menu
    editor.
  </Step>
</Steps>

## Translate your items

<Steps>
  <Step title="Open the Translations tab">
    In the menu editor, swipe to **Translations**.
  </Step>
  <Step title="Fill in each language">
    For every supported language, fill in the translated name and
    description per category and per item. You don't have to translate
    everything at once — any missing translation falls back to the
    default language.
  </Step>
  <Step title="Publish">
    Translations are part of the menu snapshot. Publish the menu to push
    them live. See [Publish and preview](/vendors/menu/publish).
  </Step>
</Steps>

## How diners switch languages

Diners see a language switcher in the menu header. Tapping it shows only
the languages **you** enabled, not all ten. Their choice is remembered in
the browser for future visits.

<Tip>
The switcher also changes the tags on items (e.g. "Vegetarian") into the
diner's language, so you don't end up with mixed-language menus.
</Tip>

## Fallback rules

- If a diner picks a language you support but the item isn't translated
  yet, Rumizi falls back to your **default language**.
- If a diner picks a language you don't support, Rumizi falls back to the
  default language for everything.

You won't end up showing blank names or descriptions.
